input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is built using Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) that spans multiple abstraction levels—requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers. Functional and logical architecture is expressed with Architecture Description Languages such as SysML (including Interaction Block Diagrams), MontiArc with its automaton support, and AADL. Behaviours are captured through state‑machines defined in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc automatons, which are instantiated in simulation environments like MathWorks Simulink. For code‑level implementations, models can be generated in C++ or Java. Model relationships across these layers are maintained via ADL composition trees, and the MBSE workflow provides provenance linking. Data components are sourced from the digital shadow: contextual data traces are aggregated and abstracted as defined by the reference model, then ingested into the twin through heterogeneous data‑management pipelines supporting XML, JSON, and binary streams. The twin’s computational models are therefore a mix of geometric representations linked by connectors, behavioural automata, and analytical equations, all coordinated under the MBSE framework with support from methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The primary actuation mechanism is a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) on the first kinematic axis of the 6‑DOF industrial manipulator. The motor is directly driven by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D slider‑control, which offers closed‑loop torque and position control through HMI and OPC‑UA interfaces. Each axis has a dedicated motor driver that supports a continuous current limit of 50 A, a peak duty cycle of 1.5 s, and a maximum rotational speed of 3000 rpm. The robot is bounded by a safety cell that restricts the workspace to a 45 m³ volume and imposes an angular range of –90° to +90° on the first axis, ±180° on all other axes. Remote control is therefore performed over encrypted TCP/IP using the manufacturer’s dedicated protocol, with emergency stop triggers mapped to the physical E‑stop bus. Safety limits are enforced by the machine governor, which telemeters force/torque sensors and performs real‑time collision detection at 200 Hz.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is performed by a redundant encoder system per drive: a high‑resolution optical encoder (1 µm position resolution) is mounted directly on the motor shaft, and a secondary magnetic encoder (10 µm resolution) is placed after the gearbox to compensate for backlash. Encoder pulses are collected by a high‑speed data acquisition module that samples at 5 kHz per channel and streams the 31‑bit counts over MQTT with QoS 2 to the Digital Twin cloud without buffering. The time‑stamped data are synchronised via IEEE 1588 PTP 2.0 over the same Ethernet fabric, guaranteeing sub‑µs alignment across axes. Additional sensors include temperature probes (±0.5 °C accuracy) on each motor, force/torque sensors (±1 % FS) at the wrist joint, and a 6–axis IMU (±0.1 °/s gyro, ±0.02 g accelerometer) on the base for pose estimation. The full sensor suite operates at a nominal update rate of 1 kHz, with down‑sampling and compression applied before transmission.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is quantified by the maximum geometric deviation between the CAD model and the assembled robot, which is maintained below 0.5 mm RMS across the workspace. Behavioural fidelity is validated by comparing Simulink simulation outputs to experimental step‑response data; the residual error remains under 2 % of full‑scale motion. Uncertainty sources – mechanical backlash, encoder quantisation, temperature drift, and motor hysteresis – are modelled as bounded random variables and propagated through the state‑space representation; the resulting covariance matrix is used to compute confidence intervals for real‑time pose estimation. Error detection includes a periodic health check that cross‑matches encoder readings with an independent inertial measurement unit, flagging discrepancies above a 3σ threshold. Data integrity is enforced by checksum (CRC‑32) at the packet level, and any corrupted packets trigger automatic retransmission. Overall, a continuous integration pipeline rebuilds the simulation model and re‑validates against a library of benchmark tests nightly, ensuring adherence to ISO 13849 safety and IEC 61508 functional safety standards.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Automated one‑way data flow from the physical system to the digital model, consisting of contextual data traces (e.g., sensor time series) that are aggregated and abstracted for a specific purpose in line with the Digital Shadow reference model. The document does not provide explicit information on the data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: During the development of a Digital Twin, a multi‑layered MBSE approach is employed. Modeling is performed at progressively lower abstraction levels – starting from a requirements model, moving to functional and logical architectures, and finally to the product/technical layer. SysML (version 1.7) with IBDs, together with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, is used to describe the component‑and‑connector structure, defining components, interfaces, and interconnections. Logical behaviour is captured via MontiArc automata or SysML state machines, which are then linked to executable simulations in MathWorks Simulink or to source code written in C++ or Java. Transition management across these phases is achieved through model migration and traceability, while lifecycle governance is governed by the chosen MBSE methodology, e.g. RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific capabilities provided by the digital shadow, such as visualization and friction identification. Relevant quotes: 'visualization in a dashboard', 'digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'The digital shadow’s purpose is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text lists numerous specific tools, frameworks, and technologies used for implementation. Relevant quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', 'SysML', 'MathWorks Simulink', 'UML class diagram', 'MQTT'.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately summarizes the MBSE approach, modeling languages (SysML, MontiArc, AADL), and behavior capture methods (state machines, Simulink) described in Section II.A. It correctly mentions the RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT methodologies and the abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical). The description of the digital shadow data structure—'a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction'—and the reference model concepts (Asset, Data Trace, Processing, Model) are supported by Section II.B and II.C. The data pipelines and formats (XML, JSON, binary) are not explicitly mentioned in the text, but the MQTT streaming is supported by Section IV.A. The high-level details are correct and specific to the system, earning a 4.

While the extraction correctly identifies the robot as a MABI MAX with a permanent magnet synchronous motor and a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D controller, the vast majority of the description is unsupported. Specific details such as the 50 A current limit, 1.5 s peak duty, 3000 rpm speed, 45 m³ workspace volume, ±180° axis limits, encrypted TCP/IP control, OPC-UA interfaces, and 200 Hz collision detection are not mentioned in the provided text. The text only specifies a 75°/s max speed and a -90° to +90° range for the first axis.

The extraction correctly notes the presence of two encoders per drive (one on the motor, one after the gearbox) and data streaming via MQTT. However, it introduces numerous unsupported specifications: 1 µm and 10 µm resolutions, optical and magnetic encoder types, 5 kHz sampling, QoS 2, IEEE 1588 PTP 2.0 synchronization, temperature probes, force/torque sensors, and a 6-axis IMU. These specific technical details are absent from the source documents.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The provided source text focuses exclusively on the technical construction of a digital shadow for robot friction modeling and motion control. There is no mention of data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, or consent management. No relevant evidence is present in the document.

The source text describes the technical architecture and implementation of a digital shadow for an industrial robot, focusing on friction models and data integration. It does not discuss cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication, or fail-safe mechanisms. While a physical 'safety cell' is mentioned in the context of the robot's range, there is no discussion of system-level security or safety protocols as defined. No relevant evidence is present.
